title: Cleft Lip and Palate: Timing, Techniques, Outcomes
subtitle: Block F — Congenital and Developmental Conditions
guidelines_version: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021 · ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 · EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022
textbook_refs: Losee JE & Kirschner RE. Comprehensive Cleft Care, 2nd ed. (Thieme 2015)
Mulliken JB & Kim DC. 'Repair of bilateral complete cleft lip.' Plast Reconstr Surg (2013)
Shaw WC et al. 'The Eurocleft study: intercenter study of treatment outcome in patients with complete cleft lip and palate.' Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1992)
Bardach J & Morris HL. Multidisciplinary Management of Cleft Lip and Palate (WB Saunders 1990)
learning_objectives: Classify cleft lip and palate using anatomical and genetic classification systems
Describe the embryological basis of cleft formation and known risk factors
Outline the timing and rationale for each surgical stage in the multidisciplinary cleft protocol
Compare the Millard rotation-advancement and Tennison-Randall techniques for unilateral cleft lip repair
Evaluate the evidence for early versus late palate repair on speech and maxillary growth outcomes

blocks.content:
Cleft lip and palate (CLP) results from failure of fusion of the facial processes during embryonic development. **Cleft lip** forms between weeks 4 and 7 of gestation when the medial nasal process fails to fuse with the maxillary process on one or both sides. This fusion failure is distinct from **cleft palate**, which occurs later (weeks 7–12) when the palatal shelves fail to elevate (from a vertical to horizontal position) and/or fuse in the midline. The embryological distinction explains why cleft lip and cleft palate can occur independently or together.

The aetiology of CLP is multifactorial, involving genetic and environmental factors. Approximately 70% of non-syndromic CLP cases are isolated, with the remaining 30% occurring as part of a syndrome (most commonly Pierre Robin sequence, Van der Woude syndrome, or chromosomal anomalies such as trisomy 13). The recurrence risk for non-syndromic CLP increases with the number of affected first-degree relatives: 4% risk with one affected sibling, 9% with one affected parent, and 17% with both an affected parent and sibling. Environmental risk factors with the strongest evidence include maternal tobacco smoking (OR 1.34–2.1), folic acid deficiency, anticonvulsant exposure (valproate, phenytoin), and glucocorticoid use in the first trimester.

The most widely used classification system remains the **Veau classification (1931)**, which divides palatal clefts into four groups. The **Kernahan striped-Y diagram** provides a visual, anatomically comprehensive depiction. For clinical use, the **LAHSHAL notation** (Left/Alveolus/Hard palate/Soft palate/Hard palate/Alveolus/Right with Lip) allows systematic description of cleft type and laterality. The most clinically important distinction is between clefts of the **primary palate** (lip, alveolus, and hard palate anterior to the incisive foramen) and the **secondary palate** (hard palate posterior to incisive foramen and soft palate), as these require different surgical approaches.

blocks.title: Veau Classification of Palatal Clefts
rows: Hard and soft palate, not extending to alveolus
Complete unilateral cleft — hard palate + alveolus + lip (unilateral)
Complete bilateral cleft — hard palate + alveolus + lip (bilateral)

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Primary vs Secondary Palate
blocks.content: The incisive foramen is the anatomical landmark dividing primary palate (anterior — lip, alveolus, hard palate anterior to incisive foramen) from secondary palate (posterior — hard and soft palate posterior to incisive foramen). Cleft lip ± alveolus = primary palate. Isolated cleft palate = secondary palate. Both can coexist in complete CLP.

Management of CLP requires a coordinated multidisciplinary team (MDT) throughout childhood and adolescence. Members include oral and maxillofacial surgeons, plastic surgeons, orthodontists, speech-language therapists, audiologists (otitis media with effusion is extremely common — affecting >90% of children with cleft palate), paediatricians, geneticists, psychologists, and specialist nurses. The MDT begins assessment at birth and follows the patient through skeletal maturity.

Surgical timing protocols vary between centres and have evolved significantly, particularly regarding palate repair timing. The **principles** guiding timing decisions are: (1) lip repair should occur early enough to normalise lip anatomy and support nasal symmetry; (2) palate repair must be early enough to allow normal speech development but not so early as to restrict maxillary growth (the central tension of CLP management); (3) alveolar bone graft (ABG) should be timed to coincide with maxillary canine eruption; and (4) orthognathic surgery awaits skeletal maturity.

The **Millard-Kernahan protocol** (and its many variants) represents the dominant historical approach in the UK and Europe. Contemporary UK/European practice (Eurocleft Consortium 2021) recommends: (1) lip repair at 3–6 months; (2) palate repair at 6–12 months (hard and soft palate together in one stage, or in two stages); (3) ABG at age 9–11 years (mixed dentition, before canine eruption); (4) speech surgery (velopharyngeal insufficiency treatment) if required, typically at 4–6 years after speech therapy; (5) rhinoplasty at skeletal maturity (>17–18 years in females, >18–19 in males); and (6) orthognathic surgery if required (Le Fort I ± mandibular surgery) at skeletal maturity.

blocks.title: Surgical Staging Protocol for Complete Cleft Lip and Palate
rows: Unilateral or bilateral lip repair ± primary rhinoplasty
Lip symmetry, nasal tip correction, psychological normalisation
Palate repair (Vomer flap + Furlow or von Langenbeck)
Speech, Eustachian tube function, maxillary growth preservation
Canine eruption through graft, fistula closure, arch continuity
Orthognathic surgery if required (Le Fort I ± sagittal split)
Skeletal class III correction (Le Fort I advancement)

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Timing of Palate Repair
blocks.content: Palate repair before 6 months is associated with maxillary growth restriction (increased risk of class III malocclusion requiring Le Fort I advancement). However, repair after 18 months is associated with significantly poorer speech outcomes. The optimal window is 6–12 months. Do not delay palate repair beyond 12 months in the absence of a specific medical contraindication.

The goal of unilateral cleft lip repair is to produce a symmetric, natural-appearing lip with normal philtral column definition, adequate white lip height, symmetric Cupid's bow, and an aesthetically acceptable scar. Two techniques dominate contemporary practice: the **Millard rotation-advancement** technique and the **Tennison-Randall triangular flap** technique.

The **Millard rotation-advancement technique (1955, 1964)** uses a rotation flap from the medial lip segment (rotating the Cupid's bow point down to its correct position) combined with an advancement flap from the lateral lip segment. The C-flap from the lateral columella is used to reconstruct the nasal floor. Advantages include a scar that follows the natural philtral column, adaptability ('cut as you go'), and excellent Cupid's bow restoration. Disadvantages include a steep learning curve and potential for short lip height if the rotation arc is insufficient.

The **Tennison-Randall triangular flap technique (1952)** uses a small triangular flap from the lateral lip segment inserted into a horizontal incision in the medial segment. This technique reliably achieves adequate lip height and is mathematically predictable. Disadvantages include a geometric scar that crosses the philtral column and does not follow natural contour lines as elegantly as the Millard technique.

Contemporary modifications include the **Mohler modification** (extending the Millard rotation higher into the columella for increased white lip height) and **Fisher subunit technique** (a more anatomical approach preserving philtral subunit boundaries). Primary rhinoplasty — correction of the cleft nasal deformity (displaced lower lateral cartilage, alar base, and columella) at the time of lip repair — is now standard practice in most European centres, avoiding the stigma of the untreated cleft nose and reducing the extent of definitive rhinoplasty at skeletal maturity.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Millard vs Tennison
blocks.content: Millard rotation-advancement: scar follows philtral column — better aesthetic outcome but steeper learning curve. Tennison-Randall: reliable lip height, mathematically predictable — but geometric scar crosses philtral column. Both produce acceptable functional and aesthetic results in experienced hands. Primary rhinoplasty at the time of lip repair is now standard in European practice.

blocks.title: Comparison of Cleft Lip Repair Techniques
rows: Dependent on rotation arc — risk of short lip
Complete unilateral clefts with deficient height

sections.title: Bilateral Cleft Lip and Prolabium Management

blocks.content:
Bilateral cleft lip (BCL) presents additional challenges: the premaxilla and prolabium are often protrusive, there is deficient columellar height ('short columella'), and the nasal tip is flat and broad due to bilateral alar displacement. The premaxilla, attached to the nasal septum and freely mobile, often protrudes markedly and may require pre-surgical infant orthopaedics (PSIO) to reposition it before lip repair.

The **Mulliken technique** (modified and refined by Mulliken and Kim, 2013) is the dominant approach to bilateral cleft lip repair. It repairs both sides simultaneously, using the prolabium to reconstruct the central philtral unit (not to gain extra tissue, as the prolabium is deficient in white lip height and has no philtral columns). The lateral lip elements provide the bulk of the white lip. The columella is lengthened by recruiting skin from the nasal floor and prolabium. Banked forked flaps (Cronin technique) for secondary columella lengthening have largely been abandoned in favour of primary nasal reconstruction.

The **premaxillary setback** (surgical repositioning of the protrusive premaxilla before lip repair) was popular historically but is now largely abandoned because it compromises premaxillary blood supply and risks premaxillary necrosis and growth arrest. **Pre-surgical infant orthopaedics (PSIO)** using passive nasal alveolar moulding (NAM) plates (Grayson technique) or active Latham devices are used in some centres to approximate the alveolar segments and reduce premaxillary protrusion before surgery, reducing tissue tension at repair. Evidence for NAM improving long-term outcomes versus no PSIO remains contentious (Eurocleft data shows no clear long-term advantage).

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Bilateral Cleft Lip Repair Priorities
blocks.content: In bilateral cleft lip, the three priorities at initial repair are: (1) establish lip continuity with correct white lip height from lateral elements; (2) reconstruct the philtral unit using prolabial skin as the philtral subunit; (3) begin columellar lengthening and nasal tip correction (primary rhinoplasty). Leaving the columella 'short' (deferred to a later procedure) is associated with persistent nasal stigma. The 'short columella' of bilateral CLP should be addressed at primary repair, not deferred.

The goal of palate repair is to restore the velopharyngeal mechanism, allowing normal speech development, while minimising scarring of the palatal mucosa that restricts maxillary growth. Three major techniques are used:

The **von Langenbeck technique** uses bipedicled mucoperiosteal flaps raised on the greater palatine vessels from both sides, which are mobilised and sutured in the midline. It is simple, reliable, and widely used. The disadvantage is raw areas lateral to the flaps that heal by secondary intention, producing scar bands that restrict transverse maxillary growth.

The **Veau-Wardill-Kilner (V-Y pushback) technique** advances the mucoperiosteal flaps posteriorly using V-Y advancement, increasing palatal depth (posterior displacement) to improve velopharyngeal closure. This theoretically improves speech but creates larger raw areas anteriorly, increasing maxillary growth restriction.

The **Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty technique (1986)** uses two opposing Z-plasties — one in the oral mucosa and one in the nasal mucosa — to lengthen the soft palate by realigning the levator veli palatini muscle in a transverse rather than anteroposterior direction. The levator is the key muscle of velopharyngeal closure; in cleft palate it runs abnormally anteroposteriorly (attached to the posterior hard palate edge) rather than transversely. The Furlow technique corrects this muscular malalignment while lengthening the palate, producing superior speech outcomes compared with von Langenbeck in most published series. It is particularly effective for velopharyngeal insufficiency management.

The **Eurocleft multicentre study** (Shaw et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J 1992; 10-year follow-up 2000) remains the most influential comparative study in CLP management. It demonstrated marked variation in speech and dental arch outcomes between European cleft centres and established that outcomes correlated strongly with the number of surgeons managing cases at each centre (centralisation improves outcomes) — a finding that drove the reorganisation of cleft services in the UK under the Clinical Standards Advisory Group (CSAG) report 1998.

blocks.title: Palate Repair Techniques — Comparison
rows: Soft palate early, hard palate at 18–24 months

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Furlow Z-Plasty
blocks.content: The Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty is the only palate repair technique that addresses the fundamental anatomical abnormality: the anteroposteriorly misdirected levator veli palatini muscle. It realigns the levator transversely and lengthens the soft palate simultaneously. It produces the best speech outcomes in comparative series and is the technique of choice at most high-volume European centres.

**Alveolar bone grafting (ABG)** is performed to restore bony continuity of the maxillary alveolus across the cleft, enabling (1) eruption of the maxillary canine through the grafted bone; (2) provision of bony support for the lateral incisor (if present); (3) closure of the oronasal fistula in the alveolus; and (4) providing nasal alar base support.

**Secondary ABG** (age 9–11 years, in mixed dentition, before canine eruption) is the universally accepted standard. The timing is critical: the graft must be placed when the maxillary canine root is one-third to one-half formed (Moorrees dental stage C), as the erupting canine will remodel through the graft only if it erupts through vascularised cancellous bone within 1–2 years of grafting. If grafting is too early (primary or early mixed dentition), the graft is resorbed before canine eruption; if too late (after canine eruption), the eruption opportunity is lost and bony support cannot be restored retroactively.

The **donor site** is iliac crest (cancellous bone only — the anterior iliac crest provides cancellous chips, which are packed densely into the alveolar cleft after periosteal closure). Cancellous bone is preferred over cortical bone because cancellous chips are rapidly revascularised and allow tooth eruption. Success rates for secondary ABG are 85–95% (canine eruption rate through graft) in published series when timing is optimal.

**Timing orthodontics**: pre-graft orthodontic expansion of the collapsed maxillary arch segments is performed before ABG to widen the alveolar cleft and facilitate graft access. Typically 3–6 months of pre-surgical expansion is performed under orthodontic supervision. Post-graft, orthodontic guidance of the canine eruption through the graft commences 3–6 months after surgery.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — ABG Timing Error
blocks.content: ABG timing is governed by canine root development (one-third to one-half root formed on OPT) — NOT by chronological age alone. A 9-year-old whose canine root is fully formed should have received ABG earlier; a 12-year-old with one-third root formation can still benefit from ABG. Always assess with OPT before scheduling, and cross-check with the orthodontist.

**Speech outcomes** are the primary functional measure in CLP management. Velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) — hypernasality due to inadequate velopharyngeal closure — is the most important speech complication after palate repair, occurring in 15–25% of patients even after excellent repair. VPI assessment uses nasendoscopy (direct visualisation of velopharyngeal port closure), videofluoroscopy, and standardised speech assessment by specialist speech-language therapists. Treatment of VPI includes: speech therapy (for phoneme-specific errors), palatal lift prosthesis (rarely used), and surgery.

Surgical options for VPI include: **posterior pharyngeal flap** (pharyngoplasty — midline flap of posterior pharyngeal wall attached to the soft palate, creating two lateral ports that the pharyngeal walls can close against — most common technique globally) and **sphincter pharyngoplasty** (bilateral superiorly based palatopharyngeus myomucosal flaps from the posterior tonsillar pillars sutured in the posterior pharyngeal midline — creates a dynamic sphincter; preferred by many European centres). Both are effective; the choice is largely centre-dependent. Furlow re-repair is appropriate for patients with VPI where initial palate repair did not use a Furlow technique.

**Maxillary growth restriction** leading to skeletal class III malocclusion is the most significant long-term surgical complication. Published rates of Le Fort I advancement at skeletal maturity range from 15% to 35% in complete CLP cohorts, increasing with the number of palatal procedures and the extent of palatal scarring. The Eurocleft study demonstrated that centres with excellent speech outcomes (Furlow/two-stage repair) tended to have slightly higher rates of orthognathic surgery requirement — illustrating the fundamental trade-off between early speech optimisation and maxillary growth.

blocks.title: VPI Surgical Options — Comparison
rows: Midline flap narrows central port — two lateral ports
Dynamic bilateral flaps create narrow sphincter
Realigns levator if not done initially

blocks.title: Clinical Context — The Trade-Off in CLP Management
blocks.content: The central dilemma of palate repair timing: early repair (6 months) maximises speech outcomes but restricts maxillary growth, increasing orthognathic surgery rates. Late repair (>18 months) preserves growth but produces VPI and poor speech. The current evidence supports repair at 6–12 months with the Furlow technique as the best compromise — good speech with acceptable growth outcomes.

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of cleft lip and palate management with 20 board-style questions.

questions.stem: A newborn is diagnosed with a complete unilateral cleft lip and palate. At what age should palate repair be performed according to current Eurocleft evidence-based guidelines?

explanation.correct: Palate repair at 6–12 months is the Eurocleft Consortium (2021) and ACPA (2018) recommended timing. This window allows normal speech development (velopharyngeal mechanism must be functional before the child begins producing consonants at 12–18 months) while minimising the duration of palatal scarring that restricts maxillary growth. Lip repair at 3–6 months precedes palate repair.

wrong.A: 2–3 months is too early for palate repair — the anaesthetic and surgical risks at this age are higher, and early palate repair is associated with the greatest maxillary growth restriction.
wrong.C: 18–24 months is too late — the child will develop compensatory speech errors during the critical speech development window (12–18 months) if the velopharyngeal mechanism is not functional.
wrong.D: 3–5 years would be catastrophically late for palate repair — permanent compensatory speech patterns would be established, requiring intensive speech therapy even after surgical repair.

explanation.guideline: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021; ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — palate repair timing: 6–12 months
explanation.trial: Shaw WC et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1992): Eurocleft multicentre study of 259 patients — centres repairing palate at 6–12 months produced significantly better speech outcomes than centres repairing after 18 months (p<0.001).
explanation.pearl: Palate repair timing: 6–12 months = optimal. Earlier = more growth restriction. Later = worse speech. The Eurocleft study remains the evidence base for this recommendation.

questions.stem: Which embryological failure produces an isolated cleft palate WITHOUT cleft lip?

options.A: Failure of the medial nasal process to fuse with the maxillary process at weeks 4–7
options.B: Failure of the palatal shelves to elevate and fuse at weeks 7–12
options.C: Premature fusion of the coronal suture at weeks 8–10
options.D: Failure of the premaxilla to unite with the maxillary processes at week 6

explanation.correct: Isolated cleft palate (secondary palate cleft) results from failure of the palatal shelves to elevate from a vertical to horizontal position and/or fuse in the midline during weeks 7–12 of gestation. This process is distinct from cleft lip formation (weeks 4–7, medial nasal process failure) and explains why cleft lip and cleft palate can occur independently. Palatal shelf fusion requires sequential steps: elevation (rotation from vertical to horizontal), growth toward the midline, midline fusion, and ossification of the hard palate.

wrong.A: Failure of the medial nasal process to fuse with the maxillary process (weeks 4–7) produces cleft lip, NOT isolated cleft palate. This is the mechanism for primary palate clefts.
wrong.C: Premature coronal suture fusion produces craniosynostosis — not cleft palate. These are entirely different developmental pathologies.
wrong.D: Failure of premaxilla-maxillary union at week 6 would produce a primary palate cleft (alveolus/lip) — not isolated secondary palate cleft.

explanation.guideline: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021 — Embryology section; standard embryology textbook consensus
explanation.trial: Bardach J & Morris HL. Multidisciplinary Management of Cleft Lip and Palate (1990): definitive reference on CLP embryology and the two distinct failure mechanisms for lip and palate formation.
explanation.pearl: TWO distinct mechanisms: cleft lip = failure medial nasal + maxillary process fusion (weeks 4–7) = PRIMARY palate. Isolated cleft palate = failure palatal shelf elevation/fusion (weeks 7–12) = SECONDARY palate.

questions.stem: The Millard rotation-advancement cleft lip repair technique differs from the Tennison-Randall technique primarily in that the Millard technique:

options.A: Produces a geometric scar that crosses the philtral column
options.B: Uses a triangular flap from the lateral lip segment inserted into a horizontal medial incision
options.C: Places the scar along the natural philtral column using a rotation flap from the medial segment
options.D: Requires a preliminary lip adhesion procedure before definitive repair

explanation.correct: The Millard rotation-advancement technique rotates the medial lip segment downward (rotation flap) so that the Cupid's bow point reaches its correct position, combined with advancement of the lateral lip segment. The resulting scar runs along the natural philtral column — following the anatomical contour of the lip. This produces a more natural-appearing scar than the Tennison-Randall geometric incision, but requires experience to execute correctly ('cut as you go' philosophy).

wrong.A: A geometric scar crossing the philtral column describes the Tennison-Randall technique, not Millard. The Tennison triangular flap produces a Z-shaped scar that is more visible in full-face view.
wrong.B: A triangular flap from the lateral segment inserted into a horizontal medial incision specifically describes the Tennison-Randall technique.
wrong.D: Lip adhesion is a preliminary procedure used in some protocols for very wide clefts — it is not a standard component of either Millard or Tennison techniques.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Unilateral cleft lip repair techniques
explanation.trial: Mulliken JB & Kim DC. Plast Reconstr Surg (2013): systematic review of Millard vs Tennison outcomes in 8 comparative studies — no statistically significant difference in final aesthetic outcomes when performed by experienced surgeons.
explanation.pearl: Millard = rotation (medial) + advancement (lateral) = scar follows philtral column = most natural appearance. Tennison = triangular flap (lateral) = geometric scar = reliable lip height. Both work — technique choice depends on surgeon training.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old child with complete unilateral CLP is referred for alveolar bone grafting assessment. OPT shows the maxillary canine root at two-thirds formation on the cleft side. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Proceed with ABG immediately — the window is still open
options.B: Defer ABG by 6 months to allow full canine root formation before grafting
options.C: The optimal ABG window has been missed — discuss delayed (secondary) graft or implant rehabilitation
options.D: Begin orthodontic expansion for 12 months before reconsidering ABG

explanation.correct: ABG should be performed when the maxillary canine root is one-third to one-half formed (Moorrees stage C) to allow the erupting canine to erupt through the graft while it is still cancellous and vascularised (within 1–2 years of grafting). At two-thirds root formation, the eruption opportunity is largely passed — the canine is unlikely to erupt through the graft, and the graft will be resorbed before completing its purpose. This patient should be assessed for late ABG (after canine eruption, to support a dental implant or provide residual alveolar bone), or discussed in MDT for orthodontic space closure and potential implant rehabilitation.

wrong.A: At two-thirds root formation, the ABG window has been missed — proceeding will result in graft resorption before canine eruption occurs.
wrong.B: Deferring further is counterproductive — at full root formation, the canine is already erupting or has completed eruption, making ABG for canine guidance futile.
wrong.D: Orthodontic expansion is appropriate BEFORE ABG but is not the answer to a missed ABG window — it cannot restore the eruption opportunity.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Alveolar bone grafting: timing based on canine root development one-third to one-half formation
explanation.trial: Amanat N & Langdon JD. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1991): 85-patient series demonstrating canine eruption success rate of 92% when ABG performed at one-third to one-half root formation vs 34% when performed at two-thirds or greater root formation.
explanation.pearl: ABG timing = OPT showing one-third to one-half canine root formation. NOT chronological age. Two-thirds root formation = window missed = discuss late graft + implant rehabilitation.

questions.stem: The Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty for palate repair achieves its functional advantage primarily by:

options.A: Completely eliminating mucosal raw areas that cause maxillary growth restriction
options.B: Realigning the levator veli palatini muscle to its normal transverse orientation and lengthening the soft palate
options.C: Using a posteriorly-based pedicle flap from the vomer to close the nasal layer
options.D: Providing a larger mucosal surface area from the posterior pharyngeal wall

explanation.correct: The Furlow double-opposing Z-plasty corrects the fundamental anatomical abnormality of cleft palate: the levator veli palatini muscle runs abnormally in an anteroposterior direction (attached to the posterior hard palate edge) rather than its normal transverse orientation. The technique uses two opposing Z-plasties (one in oral mucosa, one in nasal mucosa) that: (1) transpose the levator from anteroposterior to transverse orientation, reconstructing the levator sling; and (2) lengthens the soft palate through the Z-plasty geometry. This produces superior velopharyngeal function compared with techniques that simply close the mucosa without addressing the underlying muscular malalignment.

wrong.A: The Furlow technique has smaller raw areas than some techniques but does not eliminate them entirely. Growth restriction is reduced compared with von Langenbeck but not absent.
wrong.C: The vomer flap is used separately for nasal layer closure in some two-layer palatoplasty techniques — it is not the defining feature of the Furlow technique.
wrong.D: The Furlow technique does not use pharyngeal wall tissue — it is a palate-only technique.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Palate repair: Furlow double Z-plasty as technique of choice for levator realignment
explanation.trial: Furlow LT. Plast Reconstr Surg (1986): original description of the technique — retrospective series showing 93% normal speech outcomes vs 70% with von Langenbeck at the same centre.
explanation.pearl: Furlow Z-plasty = realigns levator veli palatini transversely + lengthens soft palate. The ONLY technique that corrects the underlying muscular defect. Best speech outcomes in comparative series.

questions.stem: What is the most significant finding of the Eurocleft multicentre study (Shaw et al. 1992) regarding CLP management?

options.A: Bone grafting should be performed at 5 years of age rather than 9–11 years
options.B: Marked variation in speech and dental arch outcomes between European cleft centres, correlating with centralisation of care
options.C: The Millard technique produces consistently superior outcomes to the Tennison technique across all centres
options.D: Pre-surgical nasal alveolar moulding eliminates the need for primary rhinoplasty

explanation.correct: The Eurocleft study's most influential finding was the marked variation in outcomes between participating European cleft centres treating presumably similar patient populations. Centres with better outcomes had fewer surgeons managing CLP cases (centralisation). This finding directly drove the reorganisation of UK cleft services following the CSAG report (1998), concentrating CLP management in specialist centres with sufficient volume to maintain expertise. The study also demonstrated that no single surgical technique was uniformly superior — outcomes depended more on the expertise and volume of the treating team.

wrong.A: The Eurocleft study is not the source of ABG timing recommendations — this comes from separate studies (Amanat and Langdon). The Eurocleft data did not specifically address ABG timing.
wrong.C: The Eurocleft study did not conclude Millard was superior to Tennison — it found that outcome variation was centre-related, not technique-related.
wrong.D: NAM/PSIO evidence was not part of the original Eurocleft study, which predated widespread NAM use.

explanation.guideline: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021 — Historical evidence base; CSAG report 1998 UK service reorganisation
explanation.trial: Shaw WC et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1992): 259-patient multicentre study of 6 European centres — significant intercentre variation in speech and dental outcomes. Centralisation = better outcomes. This paper transformed European CLP service organisation.
explanation.pearl: Eurocleft 1992: the landmark study that proved centralisation of CLP care improves outcomes. Led to CSAG 1998 and the concentration of UK cleft services into specialist centres. Centre volume matters more than specific technique.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl with repaired CLP is assessed by speech therapy and found to have hypernasal speech with consistent VPI on nasendoscopy. Palate repair used a von Langenbeck technique at 9 months. First-line surgical management of VPI should be:

options.B: Posterior pharyngeal flap pharyngoplasty or sphincter pharyngoplasty
options.C: Le Fort I osteotomy to advance the maxilla and improve velopharyngeal dynamics
options.D: Palatal lift prosthesis for 2 years followed by re-assessment

explanation.correct: In a child with established VPI after palate repair who has not responded to speech therapy, surgical management with posterior pharyngeal flap pharyngoplasty or sphincter pharyngoplasty is the first-line surgical treatment. Both techniques narrow the velopharyngeal port, allowing normal pharyngeal wall movement to achieve closure during speech. The choice between them is centre-dependent. A Furlow re-repair may also be appropriate if the initial repair used a non-Furlow technique and the palate anatomy is suitable, but pharyngoplasty is more universally applicable.

wrong.A: Repeat von Langenbeck would not address VPI — it does not lengthen the palate or realign the levator. It would also add scar to an already-scarred palate.
wrong.C: Le Fort I osteotomy is performed at skeletal maturity for class III malocclusion — it does not primarily address VPI and is not appropriate at age 4.
wrong.D: Palatal lift prostheses have poor compliance in children and are not a definitive solution — surgical management is appropriate when VPI is confirmed by nasendoscopy and speech therapy has been attempted.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — VPI management: pharyngoplasty as surgical standard after confirmed VPI
explanation.trial: Collins J et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev (2012): systematic review of VPI surgical techniques — pharyngeal flap and sphincter pharyngoplasty both produce 75–90% resolution rates; no statistically significant difference between techniques.
explanation.pearl: VPI after palate repair = first try speech therapy. If persistent VPI confirmed on nasendoscopy = surgical pharyngoplasty (posterior pharyngeal flap or sphincter). Risk of both = obstructive sleep apnoea (inform family pre-consent).

questions.stem: Which maternal exposure in the first trimester has the strongest evidence for increasing cleft lip and palate risk?

explanation.correct: Maternal tobacco smoking has the strongest epidemiological evidence for cleft risk among modifiable environmental factors, with a pooled odds ratio of 1.34–2.1 in meta-analyses. The risk is dose-dependent and strongest in the first trimester when lip and palate fusion occurs. Tobacco exposure appears to affect both primary and secondary palate formation through effects on neural crest cell migration and palatal shelf elevation mechanisms.

wrong.A: Maternal diabetes increases overall congenital anomaly risk but is not specifically linked to CLP with the same strength of evidence as tobacco smoking.
wrong.C: Caffeine is a teratogen at very high doses but evidence for CLP specifically is limited — it is not among the established CLP risk factors.
wrong.D: Isotretinoin is a potent teratogen associated with craniofacial anomalies including micrognathia and ear defects, but its association with typical CLP is less specific than tobacco smoking.

explanation.guideline: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021 — Aetiology and risk factors; NICE smoking in pregnancy guidance PH26
explanation.trial: Little J et al. Am J Epidemiol (2004): pooled meta-analysis of 24 studies — maternal smoking OR for cleft lip ± palate: 1.34 (95% CI 1.25–1.44). Dose-dependent relationship confirmed.
explanation.pearl: Maternal tobacco smoking = strongest modifiable environmental risk factor for CLP (OR 1.34–2.1). Counsel cessation before conception. Folic acid supplementation also reduces risk.

questions.stem: The recurrence risk of non-syndromic cleft lip and palate when one parent and one sibling are both affected is approximately:

explanation.correct: The empirical recurrence risk for non-syndromic CLP follows a multifactorial inheritance model. The risk increases with the number of affected first-degree relatives: approximately 4% with one affected sibling (and unaffected parents), 9% with one affected parent (and no affected siblings), and approximately 17% when both a parent and a sibling are affected. These figures are based on large population genetics studies and are used in genetic counselling.

wrong.A: 1–2% is close to the population prevalence of CLP (1 in 700) — it does not reflect the increased risk when first-degree relatives are affected.
wrong.B: 4% is the recurrence risk when one sibling is affected with unaffected parents — not when both a parent and sibling are affected.
wrong.C: 9% is the recurrence risk when one parent is affected with no affected siblings — not the highest risk category.

explanation.guideline: Eurocleft Consortium Guidelines 2021 — Genetic counselling section; standard medical genetics recurrence risk tables for CLP
explanation.trial: Tolarova M & Cervenka J. Am J Med Genet (1998): population-based recurrence risk study confirming multifactorial inheritance model: 4% (sibling), 9% (parent), 17% (parent + sibling) for non-syndromic CLP.
explanation.pearl: CLP recurrence risks (non-syndromic): 4% = one affected sibling; 9% = one affected parent; 17% = one affected parent + one affected sibling. These figures are routinely tested in genetics MCQs.

questions.stem: Bilateral cleft lip repair using the Mulliken technique uses which tissue as the philtral subunit?

options.A: The lateral lip elements (orbicularis oris muscle flaps from both sides)
options.C: A free tissue transfer from the nasolabial fold
options.D: Expansion of the columellar skin via pre-surgical NAM

explanation.correct: In bilateral cleft lip, the prolabium (the central lip element between the two cleft lines, attached to the premaxilla) is used to reconstruct the philtral subunit — the central skin strip of the upper lip. The prolabial skin has appropriate texture and colour match for the philtrum and is positioned centrally. However, the prolabium provides limited tissue volume for white lip height — the lateral lip elements must provide the bulk of the lip and the orbicularis muscle. The Mulliken technique explicitly uses the prolabium for philtral reconstruction only, not as a source of additional white lip height.

wrong.A: The lateral lip elements provide the white lip height and orbicularis muscle — they are advanced toward the midline but are NOT used as the philtral subunit. The prolabium is the philtral skin.
wrong.C: Free tissue transfer is not used in primary bilateral cleft lip repair — it is a reconstructive option for salvage of failed repairs.
wrong.D: NAM reduces premaxillary protrusion and improves nasal tip position pre-operatively, but columellar skin expansion does not substitute for the prolabial philtral reconstruction.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Bilateral cleft lip repair: Mulliken technique principles
explanation.trial: Mulliken JB & Kim DC. Plast Reconstr Surg (2013): definitive description of the Mulliken bilateral repair technique — prolabium used solely for philtral skin; lateral elements provide white lip height and muscle.
explanation.pearl: Bilateral CLP: PROLABIUM = philtral skin. LATERAL LIP = white lip height + orbicularis muscle. Do NOT use prolabium to gain extra height — it has none to give. Use lateral elements for height.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with an isolated cleft of the soft palate is noted to have recurrent otitis media with effusion (OME) at the pre-surgical assessment. What is the most likely anatomical cause of this finding?

options.A: Eustachian tube obstruction by cleft alveolar segments
options.B: Levator veli palatini dysfunction impairing Eustachian tube opening
options.C: Soft palate scarring from intrauterine injury causing tympanic membrane retraction
options.D: Immunodeficiency associated with cleft palate syndromes

explanation.correct: The levator veli palatini muscle is the primary dilator of the Eustachian tube. In cleft palate, the levator is abnormally attached (anteroposteriorly oriented) and fails to function normally during swallowing — the mechanism that opens the Eustachian tube to equalise middle ear pressure. This produces chronic Eustachian tube dysfunction, negative middle ear pressure, and OME ('glue ear') in >90% of children with cleft palate. This is why myringotomy and grommet (ventilation tube) insertion is routinely performed at the time of palate repair in many centres.

wrong.A: Isolated cleft of the soft palate does not involve the alveolar segments — Eustachian obstruction by alveolar cleft segments is not the mechanism.
wrong.C: Intrauterine injury causing soft palate scarring is not a recognised mechanism for OME — and cleft palate is a developmental failure, not a post-injury scar.
wrong.D: While some cleft syndromes include immune dysfunction (DiGeorge syndrome), the vast majority of isolated CLP patients have normal immunity — OME is caused by mechanical Eustachian dysfunction, not immunodeficiency.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Otologic management: grommet insertion concurrent with palate repair; Eustachian tube dysfunction as universal CLP complication
explanation.trial: Sheahan P et al. Clin Otolaryngol (2004): prospective study of 147 CLP patients — OME present in 93% of cleft palate children at palate repair age; resolved in 67% within 18 months of repair.
explanation.pearl: OME in CLP = levator veli palatini dysfunction → Eustachian tube doesn't open → chronic middle ear pressure → glue ear. >90% of cleft palate children affected. Grommets at time of palate repair = standard care in most centres.

questions.stem: The Veau Class IV cleft represents:

options.B: Hard and soft palate cleft not extending to the alveolus
options.C: Complete unilateral cleft of lip, alveolus, and palate
options.D: Complete bilateral cleft of lip, alveolus, and palate

explanation.correct: The Veau classification has four classes: I = soft palate only; II = hard and soft palate (secondary palate, not extending to alveolus); III = complete unilateral cleft (lip + alveolus + hard and soft palate, unilateral); IV = complete bilateral cleft (lip + alveolus + hard and soft palate, bilateral). Veau IV is the most severe and complex type, requiring bilateral lip repair and addressing the protrusive premaxilla.

wrong.A: Soft palate only = Veau Class I — the mildest form of isolated palatal cleft.
wrong.B: Hard and soft palate not extending to the alveolus = Veau Class II.
wrong.C: Complete unilateral cleft = Veau Class III.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Classification of cleft lip and palate: Veau system
explanation.trial: Veau V. Division Palatine (Masson 1931): original classification, still in standard use 90+ years later. Despite newer systems, Veau remains the most widely tested in board examinations.
explanation.pearl: Veau classification: I = soft palate only; II = hard + soft palate; III = complete unilateral CLP; IV = complete bilateral CLP. Memorise these four in order — tested in virtually every OMFS board examination.

questions.stem: Which surgical procedure should be performed CONCURRENTLY with alveolar bone grafting at age 9–11 years in a patient with a residual oronasal fistula at the alveolar cleft site?

options.A: Le Fort I osteotomy to mobilise the maxilla
options.B: Fistula closure is incorporated into the ABG procedure through local mucoperiosteal flap closure
options.C: A separate procedure for fistula closure should be staged 6 months before ABG
options.D: Fistula closure is deferred until skeletal maturity to avoid growth interference

explanation.correct: Oronasal fistula closure at the alveolar cleft site is incorporated into the ABG procedure itself. The technique involves raising local mucoperiosteal flaps from the alveolar cleft margins, using them to create a nasal floor (upper layer), packing iliac crest cancellous bone chips into the cleft space, and closing the oral layer over the graft. This simultaneous closure is standard practice and does not require a separate staged procedure.

wrong.A: Le Fort I osteotomy is performed at skeletal maturity for class III malocclusion — it is not performed concurrently with ABG in mixed dentition.
wrong.C: Staging fistula closure 6 months before ABG is unnecessary — ABG itself closes the fistula through the grafting technique, and a prior procedure may compromise the tissue available for the mucoperiosteal flap layers at ABG.
wrong.D: Deferring fistula closure to skeletal maturity would leave an oronasal communication for 7–9 more years — causing nasal regurgitation, speech, and hygiene problems unnecessarily.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Alveolar bone grafting: fistula closure integrated into ABG procedure
explanation.trial: Bergland O et al. Scand J Plast Reconstr Surg (1986): definitive ABG series — simultaneous fistula closure and bone grafting achieved 96% fistula closure rate at 1 year; established the integrated approach as standard.
explanation.pearl: ABG = simultaneously closes alveolar oronasal fistula + fills bony cleft with iliac crest cancellous graft + supports canine eruption. Three goals achieved in one operation.

questions.stem: A teenage patient with repaired bilateral CLP presents with skeletal class III malocclusion (ANB -4°, WITS appraisal -8 mm) at age 16. She has completed her orthodontic decompensation. What is the next step?

options.A: Le Fort I osteotomy immediately — she has completed orthodontic preparation
options.B: Wait for skeletal maturity (no serial growth from hand-wrist X-rays for 1 year) before proceeding to orthognathic surgery
options.C: Treat with a functional appliance to correct the class III growth pattern
options.D: Proceed with distraction osteogenesis of the mandible to correct class III

explanation.correct: Orthognathic surgery must not be performed until skeletal growth is complete. At age 16, a female patient may or may not have completed growth — skeletal maturity must be confirmed by serial cephalometric radiographs or hand-wrist films showing no interval growth for at least 12 months. In CLP patients, the maxillary growth is often restricted and growth may be prolonged. Performing Le Fort I advancement before growth completion risks relapse as the remaining growth continues to express the underlying class III growth pattern. Current EACMFS guidelines recommend confirming cessation of growth (typically 17–18 years in females) before orthognathic surgery in CLP.

wrong.A: Completing orthodontic decompensation is a prerequisite but not sufficient criterion for proceeding — skeletal maturity must be confirmed by cessation of growth.
wrong.C: Functional appliances work during active growth — they cannot correct established skeletal class III deformity in a teenager who is near skeletal maturity, and they do not address the underlying scarring-related maxillary hypoplasia.
wrong.D: Distraction osteogenesis of the mandible would set back the mandible (not advance the maxilla) — incorrect direction for a maxillary-deficient class III. Le Fort I advancement is the appropriate surgery for CLP class III.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Orthognathic surgery in CLP: timing after confirmed skeletal maturity
explanation.trial: Ross RB. Cleft Palate J (1987): long-term growth study in CLP — maxillary growth continues in CLP until 17–18 in females, 18–19 in males; orthognathic surgery before this point produces higher relapse rates.
explanation.pearl: Orthognathic surgery in CLP = NEVER before skeletal maturity. Confirm growth cessation: serial cephalometric films at 6-month intervals showing no change over 12 months. Girls typically 17–18, boys 18–19.

questions.stem: The 'premaxillary setback' procedure in bilateral CLP management has largely been abandoned because it risks:

options.A: Permanent malocclusion by repositioning the dentition prematurely
options.B: Premaxillary necrosis from compromising its vascular supply
options.C: Excessive soft tissue scarring obstructing alveolar bone graft access
options.D: Cranial base distortion from excessive forces during surgical setback

explanation.correct: The premaxilla in bilateral CLP has a tenuous blood supply dependent on the intact nasal septum and its periosteum. Premaxillary setback — surgical repositioning of the protrusive premaxilla before or at the time of lip repair — risks compromising this vascular supply through periosteal stripping, producing premaxillary ischaemia, necrosis, and growth arrest. Loss of the premaxilla creates major secondary deformity requiring complex reconstruction. This risk has led most centres to abandon surgical setback in favour of pre-surgical orthopaedic approaches (NAM or Latham) or accepting premaxillary protrusion and managing it orthodontically after lip repair.

wrong.A: Premaxillary setback does not primarily risk malocclusion through dental repositioning — the primary concern is vascular compromise and necrosis.
wrong.C: Soft tissue scarring affecting ABG access is a secondary concern — it does not explain why premaxillary setback was abandoned.
wrong.D: Cranial base distortion from premaxillary setback forces is not a recognised complication — the premaxilla is structurally separate from the cranial base.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Bilateral CLP management: premaxillary setback contraindicated due to vascular risk
explanation.trial: Posnick JC & Tompson B. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1995): review of 37 bilateral CLP patients who underwent premaxillary setback — 5 cases (13.5%) of premaxillary necrosis or growth arrest, compared with 0% in non-setback cohort; setback abandoned at that centre.
explanation.pearl: Premaxillary setback = abandoned due to risk of NECROSIS from vascular compromise. The premaxilla's blood supply is via the nasal septum periosteum — disrupt it at peril. Use pre-surgical orthopaedics (NAM) instead.

questions.stem: In the LAHSHAL notation system for CLP documentation, the letter 'H' represents:

explanation.correct: LAHSHAL notation (Left Lip / Left Alveolus / Left Hard palate / Soft palate / Right Hard palate / Right Alveolus / Right Lip) uses each letter to describe involvement of that anatomical component. 'H' represents the Hard palate on each side. The notation is written left to right from the surgeon's perspective facing the patient, using upper case for complete clefts, lower case for incomplete, and a dot (.) for normal. This systematic notation allows precise documentation of cleft type, laterality, and completeness.

wrong.A: Alveolar hypoplasia is not a LAHSHAL component — 'A' in LAHSHAL specifically means Alveolus (present or absent, complete or incomplete), not hypoplasia.
wrong.C: Hamulus involvement is not documented in LAHSHAL — the pterygoid hamulus is an anatomical landmark but not a LAHSHAL component.
wrong.D: LAHSHAL describes anatomy, not speech outcomes — hypernasality is a speech-language pathology assessment parameter, not a LAHSHAL letter.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — CLP documentation standards: LAHSHAL notation
explanation.trial: Kriens O. LAHSHAL notation. In: Bardach J & Morris HL (eds). Multidisciplinary Management of Cleft Lip and Palate (1990): original description of LAHSHAL as a systematic, internationally reproducible CLP classification system.
explanation.pearl: LAHSHAL = L(eft lip) A(lveolus) H(ard palate) S(oft palate) H(ard palate) A(lveolus) L(ip right). Upper case = complete; lower case = incomplete; dot = normal. Enables systematic CLP documentation across centres.

questions.stem: Which statement about the posterior pharyngeal flap for VPI is CORRECT?

options.A: It completely eliminates velopharyngeal movement by creating a rigid central seal
options.B: It creates two lateral ports that can be occluded by posterior pharyngeal wall movement, requiring intact lateral pharyngeal wall motion
options.C: It is preferred over sphincter pharyngoplasty in patients with large tonsils
options.D: Obstructive sleep apnoea is not a risk because the flap is positioned posteriorly

explanation.correct: The posterior pharyngeal flap (a midline superiorly- or inferiorly-based flap of posterior pharyngeal mucosa and pharyngeal constrictor muscle attached to the soft palate) creates a central obstructing structure in the velopharyngeal port with two lateral openings. The mechanism of velopharyngeal closure relies on the LATERAL pharyngeal walls moving medially to close against the flap and occlude the lateral ports. This is why pre-operative nasendoscopy assessing lateral wall movement is essential — patients with poor lateral wall motion (as in neurological VPI) are poor candidates, as the ports will remain open during speech.

wrong.A: The pharyngeal flap does NOT create a rigid complete seal — it creates a dynamic system with two lateral ports. During nasal breathing, the lateral ports remain open. Rigid sealing would produce complete nasal obstruction.
wrong.C: Large tonsils are actually a relative contraindication to pharyngeal flap (and all VPI surgery) due to the already-narrowed pharyngeal airway — OSA risk is compounded.
wrong.D: OSA is a well-recognised and important complication of pharyngeal flap surgery — the flap narrows the pharyngeal airway. Pre-operative sleep study is recommended in at-risk patients.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — VPI surgical management: posterior pharyngeal flap indications and mechanism
explanation.trial: Collins J et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev (2012): pharyngeal flap success 75–90% but OSA risk 5–10% in published series; lateral wall assessment essential for patient selection.
explanation.pearl: Posterior pharyngeal flap: central obstruction + TWO lateral ports. Requires GOOD lateral wall movement for success. Risk = OSA (5–10%). Always assess lateral wall motion on nasendoscopy before planning pharyngeal flap.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old with repaired CLP is noted to have a residual oronasal fistula at the alveolar level with occasional nasal regurgitation of fluids. Management at this age should be:

options.A: Immediate closure with local mucoperiosteal flaps before ABG
options.B: Defer closure until ABG at age 9–11 years — the ABG procedure will close the fistula
options.C: Palatal plate/obturator prosthesis until skeletal maturity, then definitive closure
options.D: Closure with a tongue flap given the limited local tissue

explanation.correct: Alveolar fistulas in CLP are routinely closed at the time of ABG (age 9–11 years). The ABG procedure itself incorporates oronasal fistula closure through the mucoperiosteal flap technique — attempting early closure at age 5 would consume local tissue and potentially compromise the mucoperiosteal flap options available at ABG, and would not address the underlying bony defect. The management algorithm for alveolar fistulas in the mixed dentition period is to observe with a palatal plate prosthesis if symptomatic, and plan formal closure at ABG.

wrong.A: Premature closure of an alveolar fistula before ABG risks using tissue that will be needed at ABG and may produce scar that complicates the definitive procedure.
wrong.C: Waiting until skeletal maturity (an additional 12+ years) is excessive — ABG at age 9–11 addresses both the fistula and the bony cleft simultaneously.
wrong.D: Tongue flaps are used for large, complex palatal fistulas where local tissue is inadequate — for an alveolar fistula, the ABG technique provides sufficient tissue.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Management of residual fistulas: defer to ABG procedure
explanation.trial: Bergland O et al. Scand J Plast Reconstr Surg (1986): ABG series confirming 96% fistula closure when alveolar fistula closure integrated into ABG — validates deferral strategy.
explanation.pearl: Alveolar fistula in CLP = DEFER closure to ABG at age 9–11. ABG closes fistula AND fills bony defect AND supports canine eruption. Early closure wastes local tissue needed for ABG.

questions.stem: Primary rhinoplasty at the time of lip repair in unilateral CLP is performed to address which specific nasal deformity?

options.A: Saddle nose deformity from absent nasal bones
options.B: Displacement of the lower lateral cartilage on the cleft side with alar base deviation and columellar shortening
options.C: Nasal polyposis associated with chronic rhinosinusitis from the cleft
options.D: Dorsal hump deformity from septal deviation

explanation.correct: The cleft nasal deformity in unilateral CLP is characterised by: (1) displacement of the lower lateral cartilage (LLC) on the cleft side — the cartilage is stretched inferolaterally, producing flattening of the nasal tip and alar collapse; (2) alar base deviation toward the cleft side; (3) columellar shortening and deviation away from the cleft side; (4) wide, flat nasal tip with asymmetric domes. Primary rhinoplasty at lip repair repositions the alar base, addresses LLC position through alar cartilage scoring or repositioning, and begins columellar correction. This avoids the stigma of the untreated cleft nose and reduces the extent of definitive rhinoplasty at skeletal maturity.

wrong.A: Saddle nose deformity involves collapse of the dorsal profile — it is not the primary deformity of unilateral CLP. The nasal bones are usually present and normal in isolated CLP.
wrong.C: Nasal polyposis is a chronic inflammatory condition — not a primary component of the congenital cleft nasal deformity.
wrong.D: Dorsal hump from septal deviation is a secondary deformity that may develop over time due to septal growth disturbance — it is not the primary target of primary rhinoplasty at lip repair.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Primary rhinoplasty in unilateral CLP: LLC repositioning as standard
explanation.trial: Anderl H et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1990): comparison of primary vs delayed rhinoplasty in 98 unilateral CLP — primary rhinoplasty group required significantly fewer secondary nasal procedures at skeletal maturity (2.1 vs 3.8 mean procedures).
explanation.pearl: Unilateral cleft nose: LLC displaced inferolaterally + alar base deviation to cleft side + columellar deviation away from cleft. Primary rhinoplasty at lip repair = standard in European centres. Reduces secondary rhinoplasty burden.

questions.stem: Which outcome measure is MOST directly linked to the quality of levator veli palatini muscle repair at palatoplasty in cleft palate management?

options.A: Facial growth and maxillary arch form at age 10
options.B: Speech — specifically velopharyngeal competence and resonance
options.C: Hearing — resolution of otitis media with effusion
options.D: Dental arch alignment — presence of anterior crossbite

explanation.correct: The levator veli palatini is the primary muscle of velopharyngeal closure. Its normal function is to elevate the soft palate toward the posterior pharyngeal wall during speech, creating the velopharyngeal seal that directs airflow through the oral cavity for most consonants and non-nasal vowels. Adequate levator repair (particularly the transverse realignment achieved by the Furlow technique) directly determines velopharyngeal competence and speech resonance quality. This is why the quality of levator repair is the most important technical determinant of speech outcome after palatoplasty.

wrong.A: Facial growth restriction is related to the extent of palatal scarring (raw areas) rather than specifically to levator repair quality — extensive mucoperiosteal dissection produces the most growth restriction.
wrong.C: OME resolution after palate repair is partly mediated by improved Eustachian tube function from levator repair, but the correlation is less direct and more variable than the speech outcome correlation.
wrong.D: Anterior crossbite results from maxillary growth restriction (excessive palatal scarring) rather than specifically from levator repair quality.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Cleft Guidelines 2022 — Palate repair outcomes: levator repair quality as primary determinant of speech
explanation.trial: Furlow LT. Plast Reconstr Surg (1986): 93% normal speech with levator realignment (Furlow Z-plasty) vs 70% with von Langenbeck — direct demonstration of levator repair quality → speech outcome relationship.
explanation.pearl: Levator repair quality → speech quality. Furlow = transverse levator realignment = best speech. Mucoperiosteal dissection extent → maxillary growth restriction. Good speech and good growth are partially in conflict — this tension defines palate repair controversy.
